This book is not written for the specialist, but for that restless, seething multitude known as "the masses." It is written for busy people, for workers, such as the shop-girl, the factory-girl, the clerk, the mechanic, the farmer, the merchant, and the busy housewife; but ministers, lawyers, and doctors may find food for thought within its covers. My heart goes out to God's secular army, composed of those who have neither time nor opportunity to go through learned treatises and scholarly essays, yet whose natures are hungering for something better than they see and hear about them. So I have tried to weave into this story the best and latest thought that has been given to the world, believing it to be what the workers most need in the performance of their daily duties, and what will help them out of bondage. SONS THE CAXTON PRESS NEW YORK. PREFACE There are few campaigns that, either in point of the immense scale upon which it was undertaken, the completeness of its failure, or the enormous loss of life entailed, appeal to the imagination in so great a degree as that of Napoleon against Russia. Fortunately, we have in the narratives of Sir Robert Wilson, British commissioner with the Russian army, and of Count Segur, who was upon Napoleon's staff, minute descriptions of the events as seen by eye-witnesses, and besides these the campaign has been treated fully by various military writers. I have as usual avoided going into details of horrors and of acts of cruelty and ferocity on both sides, surpassing anything in modern warfare, and have given a mere outline of the operations, with a full account of the stern fight at Smolensk and the terrible struggle at Borodino. I would warn those of my readers who may turn to any of the military works for a further history of the campaign, that the spelling of Russian places and names varies so greatly in the accounts of different writers, that sometimes it is difficult to believe that the same person or

### Plain English:
# Plain English Version

SONS THE CAXTON PRESS NEW YORK. PREFACE

Not many military campaigns are as interesting as Napoleon's attack on Russia. This is true because of how huge it was, how completely it failed, and how many people died. Luckily, we have detailed accounts from people who were actually there. Sir Robert Wilson was a British official with the Russian army. Count Segur was on Napoleon's staff. Both wrote about what they saw. Other military writers have also written about this campaign.

Like always, I have not included descriptions of horrible things and cruel acts that happened on both sides. These were worse than anything in modern wars. Instead, I have just given a basic outline of what happened. I do include full details about the tough battle at Smolensk and the terrible battle at Borodino.

I want to warn readers who look at other military books about this campaign. The spelling of Russian place names and people's names is very different in different books. Sometimes it is hard to tell if writers are talking about the same person or place.

As they walked they sent those quivers and thrills over their thin coats which horses can give at will; they moved their heads up and down, slowly and easily, and made their bells jangle noisily together; the bursts of sound evoked by their firm and nervous pace died back in showers and falling drops of music. All the time Elbridge swore at them affectionately, with the unconscious profanity of the rustic Yankee whose lot has been much cast with horses. The drivers of the hotel 'bus and of the local express wagon were particular friends; they gave each other to perdition at every other word; a growing boy, who had come to meet Mr. Gerrish, the merchant, with the family sleigh, made himself a fountain of meaningless maledictions; the public hackman, who admired Elbridge almost as much as he respected Elbridge's horses (they were really Northwick's, but the professional convention was that they

### Plain English:
Elbridge drove the horses down the street. As they walked, the horses shivered and trembled under their thin blankets. They moved their heads up and down slowly and easily. Their bells jangled loudly together. The sounds came in bursts as the horses moved with a firm, nervous pace. The music fell away like drops of rain. The whole time Elbridge swore at the horses in a friendly way. He cursed like a country Yankee who had spent a lot of time around horses. When he stopped at the station, he let his curses flow out naturally. He swore back and forth with the other three or four drivers who were waiting for the train. It was the only way they knew how to talk. The drivers of the hotel bus and the local delivery wagon were his best friends. They cursed at each other constantly. A young boy who came to pick up Mr. Gerrish, the merchant, with the family sleigh, also swore a lot. The local taxi driver admired Elbridge almost as much as he respected Elbridge's horses. The horses actually belonged to Northwick, but the drivers all pretended they were Elbridge's.

### Plain English:
The emperor could make kings and destroy kingdoms whenever he wanted. The old eastern part of the continent was like a dead body that just looked alive on the surface. It was slowly falling apart. Bold adventurers were fighting over the ruins and taking pieces of it for themselves. There were smaller rebellions that didn't last long and didn't change much. For example, Djezzar Pacha refused to pay taxes because he thought his fortress at Saint-Jean-d'Acre was too strong to attack. Passevend-Oglou Pacha stood on the walls of Widdin to protect the Janissaries against the new regular army that Sultan Selim created in Stamboul. But there were bigger rebellions that actually threatened the Turkish Empire and made it smaller. Czerni-Georges led one that made Serbia a free country. Mahomet Ali turned his territory in Egypt into a kingdom. And then there was Ali Tepeleni, Pacha of Janina. His story is what we're about to tell. He fought against the Turkish government for a long time. His resistance helped Greece become free again. But Ali didn't really understand how important he was to this movement. He saw it coming, but

The main change is that these pieces used to be divided into six sections, but now they're split into five chapters. That's pretty much all that's different. Other changes probably should have been made, but I haven't been healthy enough to do the serious work needed. That's also why publishing this has taken so long. I want to thank you for what you've done for me. I probably wouldn't have even tried to republish these pieces if you hadn't told me they were worth reading. I would dedicate this book to you in the old-fashioned way, but I can't find any examples of an uncle dedicating a book to his nephew. I think uncles have certain ideas about how nephews see them, so I won't explain that. There are other reasons too that I won't mention. What I will say is that even though this isn't technically a dedication, I'm using it to tell you how grateful I am. You've helped me in many ways. Your loving uncle, LESLIE STEPHEN. November 1903. PAGE INTRODUCTION 577 DISTRIBUTION OF BIRDS IN KANSAS 579 Where birds live in Kansas 581 Bird species that reach their limits in Kansas 584 BREEDING SEASONS 588 Introduction 588 How breeding seasons change 589 Geographic groups of animals 593 How evolution affects breeding times 595 What controls breeding times 598 INFORMATION ABOUT EACH SPECIES 601 THANK YOU 652 SOURCES USED 652 INTRODUCTION Scientists have been studying the birds that breed in Kansas on and off for about 75 years. Several people have written summaries about all the birds in Kansas. These writers were Goss (1891), Long (1940), Goodrich (1941), Tordoff (1956), and Johnston (1960). Except for Goss, these writers mostly just mentioned breeding birds in passing. None of them focused on where these birds live or when they breed. This book mainly looks at when birds breed in Kansas. It also covers where they live, what habitats they like, and how they relate to other bird populations around the world. We know a lot about breeding in some of the 176 bird species that breed in Kansas. For a few species, we know almost nothing. For most species, we know some things but not everything. Still, we can say useful things about many parts of breeding in these birds.

In a House of Commons that counted Pitt, Fox, Burke, and Sheridan amongst its members, he held a front rank both as a speaker and debater. Of one of his speeches in 1789 Burke said, "it equalled anything he had heard in modern times, and was not, perhaps, to be surpassed in the remains of Grecian eloquence." In 1789, Burke heard one of his speeches and said it was as good as anything he had ever heard. He said it was almost as good as the best speeches from ancient Greece. Pitt said, "Of all the people I know, Wilberforce is the best speaker." But Wilberforce had an even stronger power than his speaking ability. He could influence people from all levels of society. Friendship is how people gain influence. Wilberforce was friends with Pitt long before he started fighting against the slave trade. They stayed friends until Pitt died. This friendship gave Wilberforce a lot of personal power. It is said that one of the most impressive things in history is how close Pitt and Wilberforce were as friends. Natural History has of late become a favourite pursuit in this country; and although its progress as a study may not have been equal to the enthusiasm which it has excited, its general effect is unquestionably beneficial. Our aim of national independence, only quite recently declared by our adversaries to be "an empty dream of moonstruck idealists," has become to-day not only a practical proposition, but an accomplished fact. We have our own army, which is by no means the smallest Allied army, and we also have our own Provisional Government in Paris, recognised not only by the Allies and by all Czecho-Slovaks abroad, but even by Czech leaders in Bohemia, with whom we have since the beginning of the war worked in complete harmony and understanding. The Observatory was built by Charles II., in the year 1675--probably, observes a recent writer, "with no better motive than to imitate Louis XIV.," who had just completed the erection and endowment of an observatory at Paris. The English Observatory was fortunately placed under the direction of the celebrated Flamstead, whose name the hill, or site of the building, still retains. He was appointed astronomer-royal in 1676; but Charles (as in the case of the curious dial at Whitehall, described by us a few weeks since[1]), neglected to complete what he had so well begun: and Flamstead entered upon the duties of his appointment with instruments principally provided _at his own expense_, and that of a zealous patron of science, James Moore. ### Plain English:

The English Observatory was lucky to have a great astronomer named Flamstead in charge. The hill where the building sits is still called by his name. Flamstead was made the royal astronomer in 1676. ### Plain English:
It was hard to figure out how to make difficult ideas simple and interesting for people who don't like to think hard or do research. Finally, the old idea of telling a story to teach a lesson came to mind. This idea shows up in fables, allegories, and parables. So I imagined a remote island community. It starts out very primitive but slowly becomes very advanced. The story follows how the island's system of trading evolves. It goes from bartering to money to currency. The story also shows what happens when people use the popular money and financial ideas of today. The reader will notice from the notes in the margins that almost every silly idea about trading, money, or currency has actually happened somewhere in real history. ### Plain English:
The old lady frowned at her in the sick-room. "I never liked your mother. But she had a soft, quiet voice. That's a good thing in a woman. Why didn't you talk like that? You're doing everything you can to ruin the Carteret beauty. You're trying to look like a boy. All the Carteret women were dark-haired and beautiful, but still feminine. That's why men fell all over them, year after year." "If men fell all over me—which they don't anymore anyway—I'd kick them away. And if I were a man myself—and I really wish I was—I'd tell women to go to hell before I'd be stupid like that." "I don't like the way you talk. I don't like your voice. I don't like your short hair." "My hair isn't short." "It's bad no matter what it looks like. I don't like your dark, angry eyebrows. I read that phrase in a dumb novel. I don't like how you stand like a tough boy. It's not ladylike. I don't like your ugly suits. I've never seen you wear anything pretty or feminine." "You never will. Whether we did a good job is up to you to decide. We haven't hidden anything on purpose. Everything we say comes from careful observation, hard work, and lots of experience since childhood. We're not saying we're perfect, and we don't think our ideas are the best ones out there. But we can say that the methods in this book have worked really well. Some people might be disappointed because we don't explain how to grow new plants as thoroughly as how to take care of them. But if we had gone into all those details, it would have filled two whole books.

### Plain English:
His ship was leaving the coaling station at Cape Verde Islands. His wife suggested the name "Percival." This made him stop the ship and anchor it. He quickly wrote a letter saying he didn't want that name. "'Donald' is the name I want, Janet. I won't let the McKenzie name be ruined by an English name like 'Percival'. I won't have any Percy McKenzies in my family." He wanted to make his wife happy, since he thought she deserved it. So he added, "You can call him Percival if you really want to, but Donald comes first!" So the boy was named Donald Percival McKenzie. This is how it was written in the birth records for Glasgow, in Scotland, in 1876. The father had set his mind on the name Donald. But the mother got her way for a while. She only called her son Percival and left out "Donald" when she talked about him or to him. It was only when Captain McKenzie came home for short visits between his trips at sea.

### Plain English:
David Widger ASTORIA; OR, STORIES FROM AN ENTERPRISE BEYOND THE ROCKY MOUNTAINS By Washington Irving AUTHOR'S INTRODUCTION I visited Canada several times many years ago. During those visits, I got to know some of the main partners of the big Northwest Fur Company. They lived really well in Montreal. They basically left their doors open to visitors. At their dinners, I met other partners, office workers, and tough fur traders who worked at remote posts. These men had spent years away from cities. They had lived with Native American tribes in distant places. They told amazing stories about their travels, their hunts, and the dangerous situations they had been in with the Indians. I was young then. My imagination made everything seem more exciting. These stories made trapping and trading furs sound like a real adventure to me. I even thought about visiting the company's remote posts. I would have gone in boats that traveled up the lakes and rivers every year. One of the partners invited me. But I wasn't able to go because of other things that got in the way. I've been sorry about that ever since.
